competence_markers.description: The child does not interrupt as a default behavior — they wait for the speaker to finish before responding
The child maintains eye contact and physical orientation toward the speaker during conversations and read-alouds
The child can listen to a two-minute explanation and accurately summarize the main point
The child can follow a three-step instruction in order without reminders
The child asks clarifying questions when they do not understand something, rather than guessing
The child can narrate back key elements of a story heard during read-aloud — characters, events, and motivations
The child demonstrates awareness that listening is an active skill, not passive waiting
The child engages with narration prompts with personal reflection and genuine understanding

narration_prompts.prompt: What does it feel like when someone really listens to you?
narration_prompts.detail: How do you know they are listening? How is it different from when someone is not listening?

narration_prompts.prompt: What is the hardest part about not interrupting?
narration_prompts.detail: Why is it hard? Why is it worth doing?

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me back what I just read to you.
narration_prompts.detail: Include the important parts — who, what happened, and why.

narration_prompts.prompt: What does "What do you mean?" do for a conversation?
narration_prompts.detail: Why is it a good question to ask? When should you use it?

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me about a time you followed a multi-step instruction perfectly.
narration_prompts.detail: How did you remember all the steps?

narration_prompts.prompt: Why is listening a skill and not just something that happens?
narration_prompts.detail: What is the difference between hearing and listening?

cross_connections.description: Looking at the speaker and being still are both listening skills and presence skills. The physical foundation of 3.3.1 directly supports the listening in 3.3.3.
Not interrupting and sustained attention require the impulse control and self-regulation built in IC1.1. Without that foundation, listening discipline is very difficult to establish.
Narrating what you heard — retelling a story, repeating back instructions — builds the same organizational skills that writing develops. Listening and writing reinforce each other.
Conversation depends on listening. The child who does not listen cannot respond to what was actually said, and the conversation stalls.
Following multi-step instructions builds the same sequential processing that mathematics requires.
Listening and speaking are the two halves of communication. Clear speaking depends on having heard clearly what came before.

content.why_this_matters:
Most people think communication is about talking. It is not. The most powerful communication skill is listening — really listening, not just waiting for your turn to talk.

When you truly listen to someone, three things happen. First, you actually understand what they said — not what you assumed they said, but what they really said. Second, the person who is speaking feels respected and valued, because genuine listening is so rare that people notice it immediately. Third, you make better decisions, because you are working with real information instead of guesses.

Right now, you are going to learn the foundation: paying attention when someone speaks, not interrupting, and being able to prove that you heard them by repeating back what they said. These sound simple. They are not. Even most adults cannot do them consistently. You are going to start building these habits now, while they can become natural instead of forced.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Not interrupting** — the discipline of waiting until the other person finishes before speaking
2. **Looking at the speaker** — maintaining eye contact and physical orientation toward the person talking
3. **Repeating back** — restating what was heard to prove comprehension and allow correction
4. **Following multi-step instructions** — listening to a sequence and executing all steps in order
5. **Asking for clarification** — the habit of saying "what do you mean?" when something is unclear
6. **Listening to stories** — sustained auditory attention during read-alouds, building comprehension
content.core_content:
### Part 1: The Rule of Not Interrupting

When someone is talking, you wait. You do not start talking over them. You do not finish their sentences for them. You do not say "I know, I know" and then change the subject. You wait until they finish. Then you speak.

This is the most fundamental listening skill and one of the hardest. Your brain is fast. You often know what someone is going to say before they finish saying it. You have something you want to say, and holding it in feels almost impossible.

But interrupting tells the other person: what I want to say is more important than what you are saying. Even if you do not mean that, it is what they hear. And every time you interrupt, you miss the end of what they were saying — which might have been the most important part.

**The practice:** Starting today, when someone is talking to you, wait until they are completely finished before you say anything. If you are not sure whether they are finished, wait two more seconds. If the urge to jump in is overwhelming, take a breath and hold it gently until they stop. This will be hard at first. It gets easier.

### Part 2: Look at the Person

When someone is talking to you, turn your body toward them. Look at their face. Keep your eyes mostly on their eyes.

This is not just politeness — it actually helps you listen better. Studies show that people who look at the speaker understand and remember more of what was said than people who look away. Your brain processes spoken language better when you can also see the person's face, their expressions, and their gestures.

Looking at the speaker also tells them something important: I am here. I am paying attention. What you are saying matters to me. That message — sent through your eyes and your body — changes the quality of the conversation. People speak more honestly, more openly, and with more detail when they feel listened to.

### Part 3: Prove You Heard — The Repeat-Back

Here is the test of whether you really listened: can you say back what you heard?

This does not mean repeating word for word like a recording. It means restating the main idea in your own words. If someone tells you, "We are going to the grocery store first, then the library, and then we'll come home and make lunch," you say: "So we're going to the store, then the library, then home for lunch."

Why bother? Two reasons. First, it proves to yourself that you actually caught the information. If you cannot repeat it back, you did not fully hear it. Second, it gives the speaker a chance to correct any misunderstanding. "Actually, we are going to the library first, then the store." Without the repeat-back, you would have gone to the wrong place first.

**Practice:** After your parent or mentor tells you something — a plan, an instruction, an explanation — repeat back the key points before you act. "So you want me to clean up the blocks, wash my hands, and come to the table?" Make this a habit.

### Part 4: Following Instructions in Order

Listening is not just hearing — it is holding information in your mind long enough to use it. Following multi-step instructions is one of the best ways to build this skill.

When someone gives you three things to do, listen to all three before starting the first one. Hold them in your mind like a list. Then do them in order.

If you can only remember the first one, you were not listening to the second and third — you were already thinking about doing the first one. The skill is to stay with the speaker until they are done, hold the entire sequence, and then act.

**Start here:** Follow three-step instructions accurately. When that is easy, try four. The goal is not to memorize long lists — it is to train your brain to stay with the speaker all the way to the end.

### Part 5: "What Do You Mean?"

There is no better question in the world than "What do you mean?"

When someone says something you do not understand, you have two choices. You can pretend you understood and hope you figure it out later (this almost never works). Or you can ask: "What do you mean?" or "Can you explain that?" or "I did not understand that part — can you say it again?"

Asking for clarification is not a sign that you are slow or dumb. It is a sign that you are actually listening and you care about getting it right. The person who asks is more likely to understand than the person who guesses.

**Practice:** This week, every time someone says something you do not fully understand, ask them to explain. Notice how it feels. Notice how they respond. Most people are happy to explain — they appreciate that you care enough to ask.

### Part 6: Listening to Stories

When someone reads you a story, your job is to listen with your whole self — eyes on the reader or the book, body still, brain working to picture what is happening, to follow the characters, to understand why things happen.

After the story, you should be able to answer: Who was in the story? What happened? Why did it happen? What was the most interesting or surprising part?

Story listening is not passive. Your brain is working hard — building pictures, tracking characters, predicting what will happen next, making sense of motivations. This is the same brain work that listening requires in every conversation, every instruction, every lesson. Story time is training time.
content.formative_practices:
### Study 1: The No-Interrupting Standard [Ongoing — Backbone of the Packet]
The mentor maintains a consistent expectation: the child does not interrupt. When interruptions happen, the correction is gentle and immediate: "I was still talking. Wait until I finish, and then you can speak." Over weeks, the habit shifts from correction-driven to internalized.

### Study 2: The Repeat-Back Practice [Ongoing]
After giving instructions, sharing information, or reading a passage, the mentor asks: "What did I just say?" or "Tell me back what you heard." This is done naturally throughout the day, not as a formal exercise. The habit of confirming comprehension becomes automatic.

### Study 3: Multi-Step Instruction Games [Weekly]
The mentor gives increasingly complex multi-step instructions. The child listens to all steps, repeats them back, then executes in order. Start with three steps; build to five. Make it playful — treasure hunts, cooking steps, building projects.

### Study 4: Story Listening and Narration [Ongoing]
During daily read-aloud time, the child listens attentively and then narrates back what they heard. The narration should include characters, events, and the child's own interpretation. This is the Charlotte Mason method applied to listening development.

### Study 5: The Clarification Challenge [Mid-Packet]
For one week, the child keeps count of every time they ask "What do you mean?" or a similar clarification question. The goal is to increase the frequency — to build the habit of asking rather than guessing.

### Study 6: Listening Observation [Late Packet]
The child observes a conversation between two adults (with their knowledge) and reports: Who was listening well? How could you tell? Who was not listening well? What were they doing instead? This builds meta-awareness of listening behavior.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Not Interrupting
**Developing well:** The child waits for the speaker to finish before responding. This is the default, not a special effort.
**Developing slowly:** The child interrupts frequently despite corrections.
**Diagnostic:** Are you correcting every interruption, or only sometimes? Inconsistent correction slows habit formation.

### 2. Demonstrated Comprehension
**Developing well:** The child can repeat back the main point of a two-minute explanation. Story narrations include key characters, events, and motivations.
**Developing slowly:** The child cannot summarize what they just heard. Narrations are vague or incomplete.
**Diagnostic:** Is the material age-appropriate? A child who cannot summarize a complex explanation may be perfectly capable of summarizing a simpler one. Adjust the complexity and build up.

### 3. Following Instructions
**Developing well:** Three-step instructions are followed in order without reminders.
**Developing slowly:** Only the first step is completed; the rest are forgotten.
**Diagnostic:** Is the child actually listening to all three steps, or acting on the first one immediately? Train them to hear all steps before starting.

### 4. Asking for Clarification
**Developing well:** The child asks "What do you mean?" when they do not understand, without embarrassment.
**Developing slowly:** The child either pretends to understand or says nothing when confused.
**Diagnostic:** Have you made it safe to ask? If asking for clarification has ever been met with impatience, the child learns not to ask. Celebrate the question.
mentor_guide.common_pitfalls:
### Not Modeling Listening
The most common failure. If the child sees adults interrupting each other, checking phones during conversations, and giving half-attention, no amount of instruction will override the model.

### Accepting Incomplete Narration
"It was about a dog" after a ten-minute story is not adequate narration. Push for detail: "Tell me more. What happened to the dog? Why?" The child who is allowed to narrate vaguely does not build the listening depth that detailed narration requires.

### Making Interruption Correction Feel Punitive
"Don't interrupt!" delivered with frustration teaches the child that interrupting makes you angry. "I was still talking — wait until I finish and then you can share" teaches the child the rule. Keep the tone matter-of-fact.
